title: What is a Fixed Deposit (FD)?
meta_description: FD guide 2026 — current interest rates 6.5-7.5%, types of FD, tax on FD interest, premature withdrawal penalty, and FD vs PPF vs mutual fund comparison.

hero.one_liner: The simplest and safest investment in India — deposit a lump sum for a fixed period and earn guaranteed interest from 6.5% to 7.5% per annum

sections.heading: Overview
content: A Fixed Deposit (FD) is the most straightforward investment: you deposit a lump sum with a bank or NBFC for a fixed period (7 days to 10 years), and the bank pays you a guaranteed interest rate for that duration. Your principal is 100% safe and the interest is predetermined — no market risk, no fluctuation, no surprise.

Current FD interest rates (2026): Major banks offer 6.5-7.5% for general customers and 7-8% for senior citizens. Small finance banks and NBFCs offer slightly higher rates (7.5-9%) but with marginally higher risk. SBI, HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank rates are in the 6.5-7.0% range for 1-3 year deposits.

FD interest is fully taxable at your income tax slab rate. This is the biggest disadvantage of FDs. If you're in the 30% tax bracket, your effective return after tax on a 7% FD is only about 4.8%. For tax-efficient alternatives, consider PPF (7.1% tax-free) or ELSS mutual funds. However, for short-term parking (1-3 years), FDs remain the safest option.

Types of FDs: Regular FD (general), Tax Saver FD (5-year lock-in, 80C benefit), Senior Citizen FD (higher rate), Recurring Deposit (monthly deposits), Flexi FD (linked to savings account for auto-sweep), and Corporate FD (from NBFCs — higher rate but higher risk).

rows.label: Tenure
rows.value: 7 days to 10 years. Choose based on your goal timeline.

rows.label: Tax
rows.value: Interest is fully taxable at slab rate. TDS deducted at 10% if interest >₹40,000/year (₹50,000 for seniors).

rows.label: Premature Withdrawal
rows.value: Allowed at most banks with 0.5-1% interest rate penalty. Some banks charge additional penalty.

rows.label: Insurance
rows.value: DICGC covers up to ₹5 lakh per depositor per bank (principal + interest combined)

rows.label: 80C Benefit
rows.value: Only 5-year Tax Saver FD qualifies for 80C deduction. Regular FDs do NOT get 80C benefit.

sections.heading: FD vs PPF vs Mutual Fund vs RD — Comparison
How to Maximize FD Returns
content: 1. Ladder your FDs: Instead of putting ₹10 lakh in one 5-year FD, split into ₹2.5 lakh each in 1-year, 2-year, 3-year, and 5-year FDs. As each matures, reinvest at the current rate. This gives you better liquidity AND captures rate increases.

2. Compare rates across banks: Rates vary significantly. Small finance banks like AU, Equitas, and Ujjivan often offer 0.5-1% higher than SBI/HDFC. For amounts up to ₹5 lakh, the DICGC insurance covers you even at smaller banks.
3. Senior citizens: Always open FDs in the senior citizen's name. The extra 0.25-0.50% rate adds up significantly. On ₹10 lakh for 5 years, an extra 0.5% means ₹25,000 more interest.

4. Use Form 15G/15H to avoid TDS: If your total income is below the taxable limit, submit Form 15G (below 60) or Form 15H (senior citizens) to the bank. This prevents TDS deduction on FD interest — you get the full interest without waiting for a tax refund.

5. Consider corporate FDs for higher returns: Bajaj Finance, Mahindra Finance, and other AAA-rated NBFCs offer 7.5-8.5% on FDs. These are NOT DICGC-insured, so only invest with AAA/AA+ rated companies and keep amounts reasonable.

steps.title: Open FD online or at bank branch
steps.description: Most banks allow online FD opening through net banking or mobile app — takes 2 minutes. Select tenure, amount, and interest payout option (monthly/quarterly/at maturity).

steps.title: Choose cumulative or non-cumulative
steps.description: Cumulative: Interest added to principal, paid at maturity (better for growth). Non-cumulative: Interest paid monthly/quarterly (better for regular income — useful for retirees).

steps.title: Submit Form 15G/15H if applicable
steps.description: If total income is below taxable limit, submit to avoid TDS. Available as online submission in most bank portals.

steps.title: Auto-renewal or manual at maturity
steps.description: Set auto-renewal to avoid missing maturity date. Or set a reminder to review and decide — renew at same bank or move to a higher-rate bank.

faqs.question: Is FD interest taxable?
faqs.answer: Yes, fully taxable at your income tax slab rate. If you're in the 30% bracket, you pay 30% + cess on all FD interest. TDS is deducted at 10% if your annual FD interest across all branches of a bank exceeds ₹40,000 (₹50,000 for seniors). You claim the actual tax or refund when filing ITR.

faqs.question: What happens if the bank fails?
faqs.answer: DICGC (Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation, a subsidiary of RBI) insures deposits up to ₹5 lakh per depositor per bank. This covers principal + interest combined. So if you have ₹6 lakh in a bank that fails, you get back ₹5 lakh. To stay safe, don't keep more than ₹5 lakh in FDs at any single bank.

faqs.question: Can I break my FD before maturity?
faqs.answer: Yes, premature withdrawal is allowed at most banks with a penalty of 0.5-1% reduction in the applicable interest rate. Some banks may also charge an additional flat penalty. Tax Saver FDs (5-year lock-in) CANNOT be prematurely broken — that's the lock-in condition for 80C benefit.

faqs.question: FD vs savings account — where should I keep emergency fund?
faqs.answer: Emergency fund (3-6 months expenses) should be in a combination: Keep 1 month expenses in savings account (instant access), and 2-5 months in a sweep/flexi FD or liquid mutual fund (accessible in 1 day, earns more than savings). This way you earn decent returns while maintaining liquidity.

faqs.question: Can NRIs open FDs in India?
faqs.answer: Yes. NRIs can open NRE FD (Non-Resident External — tax-free in India, repatriable) or NRO FD (Non-Resident Ordinary — taxable, limited repatriability). NRE FDs are very popular among NRIs as the interest is completely tax-free in India — rates of 6-7% on USD-denominated returns are attractive.

disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only. Consult a qualified financial advisor before making investment decisions.
